cards.text: I once drove around for eight months with a shovel and a tarp in the trunk, for errands.
I once put a mannequin on my hometown's water tower, and the paper still calls it unexplained.
I once paid a tradesman in cash to never mention what he found in my house.
I once caused a breakup and then let both halves cry to me about it, separately, for months.
I once ghostwrote the fight texts for both sides of the same couple's argument.
I once accepted an award meant for someone with my exact name and gave brief remarks.
I once told a lie in a eulogy and was thanked afterward for that exact part.
I once timed how long it takes to drag a rolled-up rug from my bedroom to my car.
I keep a folder of screenshots of one person in this room's worst-ever messages, sorted by year.
open_slots: what you are saving them for

cards.text: I once told this whole group I was hospitalized that week. I was in a hotel, alone, and fine.
open_slots: who still checks in about it

cards.text: I once joined the search party for a neighbor's missing cat knowing exactly what I had done.
I once made out with someone at a funeral reception, and we agreed the grief made it not count.
I once invented a pet to get out of things, and the lie grew until the pet had to die.
I once said something coming out of anesthesia that the nurse refused to repeat back to me.
open_slots: your best guess at what you said

cards.text: I once sent an anonymous note that ended a couple's nine-year relationship.
I once reported a coworker for the exact thing I had done the week before.
I once realized mid-hookup that I personally knew their partner, and I did not stop.
I once got dismissed from jury duty using a performance I had rehearsed the night before.
I once faked a talent to impress a room and have since been booked to perform it three separate times.
I once invented a fake tradition as a joke, and someone in this room has now observed it, sincerely, for years.
I once started laughing at a funeral, had to be walked out, and have never told anyone what set me off.
I once found something in a date's bathroom that should have ended everything, and I chose to call it quirky.
I once secretly recorded an entire family dinner, start to finish, and I still have the file.
I once sat in a parked car outside a building for three hours, waiting for someone who deserved it.
I once worked food service and adjusted a rude customer's order in a way the health department would want to know about.
open_slots: what they did to earn it

cards.text: I once testified in small-claims court, and my version of events was, legally speaking, a creative work.
I once ended a friendship with an itemized invoice, late fees included.
I once conducted an entire breakup in HR language and treated their questions as an exit interview.
I once drafted a sincere apology letter, thought better of it, and mailed a very different kind of letter instead.
I once ended a relationship by moving all my stuff out while they were at work.
I once gave a tearful public toast in honor of someone I was actively feuding with.
I once let a stranger believe I worked somewhere and stayed in the role long enough to make a real decision.
I once ran a fake-profile loyalty test on a partner, and I've never told anyone how it ended.
I once scheduled two first dates at the same place on the same night, back to back, purely for efficiency.
I once tried to sneak out of a hookup's place before sunrise and set off their house alarm.
open_slots: what you did when it blared

cards.text: I once exited a hotel at 7am through a business conference in last night's outfit, and I did not hurry.
I once lied to an ER nurse about how I got injured, because the truth involved a person I was not supposed to be with.
I once slept with a former teacher of mine, years later, and called it closure.
I once told everyone I was traveling for work and spent four days in a hotel twenty minutes from home.
open_slots: what you did for four days
the trip story you kept up

cards.text: I once dated someone mostly because a person I can't stand loved them first.
I fought a departing roommate for custody of a $30 rice cooker, purely because losing it would gut them.
I once went through the medicine cabinet at a house party and did not leave it the way I found it.
I once started a rumor about myself at work to bury a worse truth.
I once got paid to be a stranger's job reference and answered the call in character.
I once gave a mall cop a detailed false statement to protect someone I'd met an hour earlier.
I once called out sick with a story so elaborate that my return required props.
I once called in a noise complaint on a party I was still at.
I once faked a fainting spell to escape a conversation and committed all the way through the paramedics arriving.
I once clipped a power pole with a rented truck, then stood in the news footage as a concerned neighbor.
Police once knocked about screaming at my place. It was me, alone, losing an argument with a wasp.
I once faked being hypnotized on stage and have let the story be retold wrong for years.
I once recognized someone I'd partied with in a true-crime documentary, and my first emotion was not the correct one.
I once maintained a months-long feud with a child, and described it to other adults as a personality clash.
I once got a formal talking-to from a referee at a children's sporting event where I knew none of the children.
I once held a complete funeral for a pet that was not mine, without the owner's knowledge.
I once kept a wild animal in my home for days, and it had a name and a feeding schedule before anyone intervened.
I have performed mouth-to-mouth on an animal that was not mine and was not a mammal.
I once ate something at a friend's family dinner that I later learned had been found, not bought.
I once performed a full candle-lit ritual from a book to curse a specific person, and I tracked the results.
I once knocked a small town's festival parade float into a ditch, and I cannot go back there.
I once put real money on the outcome of an amateur event, using inside information I was not supposed to have.
I once faked an illness to get out of something enormous, then had to maintain symptoms for weeks.
I once told one specific relative that Thanksgiving was canceled so they wouldn't come, and I wasn't even the host.
I once heard a stranger's darkest secret because they mistook me for someone else, and I never corrected them.
I watched someone in this room pocket a lighter at a gas station, and they still don't know I saw.
A friend's plant died under my care and I replaced it with an identical one. That was two years ago.
open_slots: how close they came to noticing

cards.text: I once let someone in this room take the blame for a thing I did, and it is now far too late to confess.
I once lied to the parents of someone in this room to cover for them, and the lie has outlived the thing it covered.
I once watched someone in this room walk directly toward a glass door and made the choice not to say anything.
I once planted a fake rumor in this friend group purely to measure how fast it would come back to me.
I once told a stranger a long story about someone in this room, and mid-story learned the stranger knew them.
I once had permission to be in exactly one room of a house belonging to someone here, and I toured several.
I once slept in my car outside the home of someone in this room instead of knocking, and they've never known.
I once recorded a voice memo of someone in this room without them knowing, and I've replayed it more than ten times.
I once snooped a bathroom cabinet at the home of someone in this room and brought the whole shelf down into the sink.
I keep a mental ranking of everyone in this room by who I'd call from jail, and one night it nearly got used.
I once gave the full name of someone in this room as my own to an authority figure.
open_slots: what it is now attached to

cards.text: I once retold a story someone in this room told me in confidence, on a date, as if it happened to me.
I once ate the clearly-labeled food of someone in this room and then helped them look for it.
I once snuck out of a party someone in this room threw, then invented a full emergency from the sidewalk outside.
Someone in this room spent over a year saved in my phone under a fake name, and the reason still holds.
cards.anchor: a year under a fake name

cards.text: I once broke something I borrowed from someone in this room and returned a replacement without a word.
I once overheard two people in this room talking about me and stayed hidden until they finished.
I once threw up somewhere so public that I joined the crowd speculating about who did it.
I once clogged a toilet at a house party, and what I did next took twenty minutes.
I once let my roommates hunt a mystery smell for two weeks while knowing exactly what and where it was.
I once dropped my retainer in a public restroom and made a decision I stand by.
I once farted in a silent group setting and successfully pinned it on a specific innocent person.
I once threw a real punch at a company holiday party and stayed for the rest of the party.
cards.anchor: stayed for the rest of it

cards.text: I once started CPR on someone before realizing they were just asleep.
I threw my back out re-enacting a wrestling move alone in my kitchen. The chiropractor heard 'hiking.'
open_slots: how long you kept the hiking story

cards.text: I once swallowed something that was not food, as an adult, and quietly monitored the situation for days.
I once broke into my own house and later heard the police read a neighbor's description of me.
I once caught a bat in my apartment with my bare hands because calling someone felt like losing.
I once tested whether I could fully fit inside my own checked suitcase, and not just the one time.
I once fought a goose over food, lost, and had to be walked away by a stranger.
I once sat through a stranger's funeral because I picked the wrong room, and I signed the guest book on the way out.
I once let a raccoon keep living in my car for a whole weekend because I was too scared to evict it.
I once spent over a hundred dollars losing a war against one specific squirrel.
I once was asked to deliver the eulogy at a pet funeral for an animal I'd met twice.
I once lost real money betting on a children's soccer game.
I once got escorted off a mini-golf course as a sober adult, in front of two birthday parties.
I once had to take a court-ordered class and got kicked out of that too.
I once accidentally stole something and smuggled it back onto the shelf a week later instead of just explaining.
I once climbed into a public dumpster in formal wear to dig out something I'd thrown away an hour earlier.
I once accidentally ruined a stranger's restaurant proposal, and they did not get engaged that night.
I once hid inside a clothing rack to avoid someone I know, and they stood there saying my name.
I once fought my own cousin at a graduation dinner and now book that restaurant under a fake name.
I once let my family believe our car dent was a hit-and-run for eleven years.
I once let someone I grew up with serve a summer-long grounding for something I did.
I once received a formal HOA complaint about what I was doing in my own yard at 3am.
I once let a door-to-door knife salesman do the entire demo because I was lonely, and I own the knives.
I once ended a three-month roommate standoff over a single unwashed pan by throwing the pan away.
I once rigged the apartment chore chart and ran the scam undetected for an entire lease.
I once taped a hand warmer to the thermostat in January and let the whole house believe the furnace was dying.
I once held a neighbor's misdelivered package hostage for a week over how they park.
I once hid a roommate's blender for six weeks over an argument, and watched them buy a new one rather than ask me.
I once kept up a pointless lie for an entire friendship because the window to confess closed in week one.
I once RSVP'd yes to a wedding, booked the travel, and spent that weekend at a water park instead.
I once helped plan a wedding I was privately certain should never happen.
I once broke the toilet at a partner's parents' house on the first visit and chose to say absolutely nothing.
I once let a friend plan our shared lease for months while I had a signed job offer in another city.
I once won an argument by citing a study I'd invented on the spot, and then they asked me to send it to them.
I have let this friend group celebrate my birthday with a food I quietly hate, three years running.
I once wore an outfit to a big event and returned it the next morning with the tags re-attached.
I once applauded at the wrong moment in a theater, then applauded harder to sell it as intentional.
I have a second grocery loyalty account because the first one knows too much about me.
I once let a barista call me the wrong name for two years rather than correct her one time.
I once pretended not to see a coworker waving at me across a parking lot, twice, in one week.
open_slots: what you did with your face

cards.text: I have described someone in this room to my family as a coworker for four years. They are not.
I once voted against someone in this room in a group decision, then helped them work out who did.
I once cost someone in this room real money and let them believe it was a bank error.
I once skipped something that mattered to someone in this room, and the emergency I cited was a nap.
I once found a wallet with cash and ID in it and returned exactly one of those two things.
I keep a saved draft of a message to someone in this room that would end it, and I still edit it.
open_slots: what would make you send it

cards.text: I once borrowed a car from someone in this room to move a piece of furniture that was not mine.
There is one person in this room I would name immediately under real pressure, and I made peace with it.
open_slots: what would make you name them

cards.text: I once took credit at work for something that got a quieter person moved to another team.
I once returned a broken appliance to a store using someone else's receipt, in their name.
open_slots: what you said at the counter

cards.text: I once cancelled a plan by inventing a death, and now I have to keep track of who I killed off.
I once took cash from a relative's dresser for two years and let a home aide be suspected.
I once let a friend go to court over something I did, and I sat in the gallery for the whole hearing.
I once emptied a joint account I shared with a sibling and let the bank take the blame for a month.
I once wrote a reference letter that quietly ended someone's shot at a job I had also applied for.
I once let my parents pay for something for two years that I had already been reimbursed for.
open_slots: what you did with the money

cards.text: I once read a dying relative's will early, and I adjusted my behavior for the rest of that year.
I once let a friend pay off a debt that had already been forgiven, and I kept the difference.
I once attributed a sentence to a mutual friend that they never said, and it followed them for years.
I once let a friend take a job I knew was about to be cut, because I wanted the one they left.
cards.anchor: the job about to be cut

cards.text: I once gave a grieving family a comforting detail about their person's last day. I was not there.
